### Post by Eleaf on 2005-11-12
haha... Hello everybody! I have been looking for a while now, but I haven't really been able to find what I'm looking for. I'm looking for a calculator that can do very large calculations. All of the calculators that I have used for linux, including command line ones get to where it goes past about 10 to the 20th it just shows a bunch of letters! 

I'm wondering if there are any calculators that you know of out there that can handle large numbers.  Thanks! :p

---

### Post by aysiu on 2005-11-12
Have you done a search in Synaptic (Name **and Description**) for the word *calculator*?

### Post by carbon-12 on 2005-11-12
Try the python interpreter!

josh@PhantomAMD:~$ python
Python 2.4.2 (#2, Sep 30 2005, 21:19:01)
[GCC 4.0.2 20050808 (prerelease) (Ubuntu 4.0.1-4ubuntu8)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.```

>>> 99**9999

>>>

```

### Post by ivanjs on 2005-11-13
[QUOTE=Eleaf]I'm wondering if there are any calculators that you know of out there that can handle large numbers.  Thanks! :p[/QUOTE]

Man, have you tried bc from the command line? I just calculated PI to 25,000 places and it showed all 25,000 places (click [HERE](http://www.geekronomicon.com/?q=node/79) to read about it and try the shell script I wrote to time your result).

John

[EDITED]-Oops! Need to read better before posting-didn't see your 10^20th comment.

### Post by posco on 2005-11-13
You might try Maxima or Octave. What are you calculating anyway? Stock market predictions? :) 

Posco

### Post by nagilum on 2005-11-14
The two command line calculators 'dc' and (as ivanjs already wrote) 'bc' are arbitrary precision calculators and should work fine. Never tested them with such big numbers though.

---

### Post by Fhernd on 2009-03-16
Hi Aleaf!

I recommend you Maple v.12. It's avaible for Linux!

Official site: [http://www.maplesoft.com/](http://www.maplesoft.com/)

### Post by ssam on 2009-03-16
> **carbon-12 said:**
> Try the python interpreter!



i have these in my .bashrc
```

alias pc='python -ic "from __future__ import division; from math import *"'
alias pcn='python -ic "from __future__ import division; from math import *;from pylab import *"'

```

so i can do

```

sam@oberon:~$ pc
>>> 3/2
1.5
>>> sin(pi/2)
1.0

```

---

### Post by jpkotta on 2009-03-22
I use wcalc.  You can set the precision with \p.  It seems like it doesn't do much beyond \p 1024.

---

### Post by cholericfun on 2009-03-22
qalculate seemes like  a pleasant little cal, not sure what youre trying to achieve here thou,

what are you gonna do with 20 decimals apart from going "whhoooaaa" ???
not trying to be "smart" but a bit more detail on what ond why your trying to produce these numbers might be of help.
PS
any standard calculator could give you parts of the result as long as you do the right formla - all else is either round off or ... very specific in that it does higher accuracies

---

### Post by Mrkluky on 2009-05-28
I'm going to love that! So much apreciated!

### Post by Indubitableness on 2009-07-03
I know I'm late to the party but I have a suggestion.

VTI (Virtual TI) works well using wine. Although it's been giving me trouble when I try to launch it from the command line it works perfectly when you just doubleclick the EXE. You can get the OS file for VTI directly from Texas Instruments at [http://education.ti.com](http://education.ti.com) and you get the emulator at [http://www.ticalc.org](http://www.ticalc.org)

This emulator will not do TI-89 Titanium or the V200. I usually use it with the TI-89 OS but it will run TI-83, I BELIEVE it will run TI-83+ as well, but probably not the TI-84.

If nothing else works, and especially if you are familiar and comfortable with TIs, this would be an excellent bet.

That being said I'm not even using it because I can't figure out how to make the dang number keys on the keyboard control the input (gotta click on all the buttons to do any math) and it keeps giving me **** when I try to launch it fromt he command line (so I can't make any shortcuts or aliases to launch it)

---

### Post by NoBugs! on 2009-10-18
Emulating a ti 89 would be cool, but I think you need a rom-dump, not just the operating system from TI. I think the only way to legally do that is if you have that model ti calculator?

If you want to do advanced math like the ti-89+, wxmaxima and maxima and k3dsurf are good open source programs for that.

---

### Post by lethalfang on 2009-10-19
> **cholericfun said:**
> qalculate seemes like  a pleasant little cal, not sure what youre trying to achieve here thou,

what are you gonna do with 20 decimals apart from going "whhoooaaa" ???
not trying to be "smart" but a bit more detail on what ond why your trying to produce these numbers might be of help.
PS
any standard calculator could give you parts of the result as long as you do the right formla - all else is either round off or ... very specific in that it does higher accuracies

A while ago, I was looking for MATLAB codes that can distinguish numbers that are 20 decimal places apart because I needed to do a bunch of the following operations:
(1-x)^N, where x is really close to zero, and N is a large number. 

Anyway, MATLAB is capable of arbitrary precision arithmetic, but it's so slow I approximated those results with exp(-x*N) instead, but that's one reason to need high precision.

---

### Post by InfernalNeutrino on 2009-10-19
+1 for python shell with the aforementioned aliases. Or better yet, use [ipython]("http://ipython.scipy.org/moin/") since it has introspection and other fun things that help you out when using some of the fancier math libraries. I use both, and like them a lot.

---

### Post by meng1usa on 2010-09-24
vote for speedCrunch

input syntax are highly flexible, ie, you can simply type in
2e4, 3e-5, 5**4(or 5^4)
all physical constants and user defined variables are list on side panel.
easy to config.

love it.

---

### Post by addy digit on 2010-10-06
Well, it depends on what classes you take in college. For the most part, the TI-84 Plus SE is a good choice for general math classes, even in college. But if you plan on majoring in either Math, Physics, or Engineering, you may need a CAS-level calculator like the TI-89 for the more rigorous coursework.

---
